mnemonics.title: PETS Remember Terminals
mnemonics.content: P-E-T-S stands for Production rules have Elements: Terminals, Start symbol. Remember - Terminals are the 'end points' you can't expand further, like the actual words in a sentence.

mnemonics.title: Left Right Left for Derivations
mnemonics.content: LRL - Leftmost derivation builds from Left to Right, Rightmost derivation builds from Right to Left. Think of reading a book: L2R for leftmost (like English), R2L for rightmost (like Arabic).

mnemonics.title: CNF - Chop Normally For
mnemonics.content: CNF (Chomsky Normal Form): Production rules must have either exactly 2 non-terminals on RHS or exactly 1 terminal. Think 'Chop' - cut everything to size 2 or 1.

mnemonics.title: GNF - Go Now For
mnemonics.content: GNF (Greibach Normal Form): All productions start with a terminal. Remember 'Go' - the terminal goes first, like 'Go forward'!

mnemonics.title: CYK - Check Your Knowledge
mnemonics.content: CYK Algorithm: C(o)cke-Younger-Kasami - used to parse CFGs. Remember 'Check Your Knowledge' to see if a string belongs to the language.

mnemonics.title: PDA - Push Down Always
mnemonics.content: Pushdown Automata - the machine that recognizes CFLs. Think 'Push Down Always' - the stack keeps pushing down, growing deeper.

mnemonics.title: S - Start at Center
mnemonics.content: In parse trees, S (Start symbol) is always at the root/center. Think 'S for Start and Sun - at the center of everything.'

mnemonics.title: Chomsky's Four Friends
mnemonics.content: Type 0 (Unrestricted) → Type 1 (Context-Sensitive) → Type 2 (Context-Free) → Type 3 (Regular). Remember: Each type adds more restrictions, like narrowing down choices.

acronyms.usage: Used when writing grammars where left side has exactly one non-terminal (like S → aSb)
The set of strings generated by a CFG - use when determining if a language can be parsed by a PDA
Use when converting grammar for CYK parsing - RHS must be exactly 2 non-terminals OR 1 terminal
Use when you need parsing to start with terminal - useful for top-down parsers
Use when recognizing/parsing context-free languages - has infinite stack memory
Use when parsing - dynamic programming algorithm for membership in CFL
When writing production rules A → aB, the RHS is 'aB'
When writing production rules A → aB, the LHS is 'A'
In CYK algorithm - explores all possibilities level by level
CYK is O(n^3) - not NPC, but grammar ambiguity is undecidable

visualTips.visualization: Picture an upside-down tree - root at top (S), branches going down. Leaves are terminals (actual words). Each branching is a production rule applied.
Imagine two different tree structures for the same sentence - like 'I saw the man with the telescope' (did I or the telescope have it?). Two trees = ambiguous.
Think of a snake eating its own tail - S → Sα keeps looping back to itself on the left side. Dangerous for parsers!
Visualize a stack of plates - push adds plates on top, pop removes from top. The PDA uses this to remember opening/closing brackets like ( ).
Think of cooking recipe - start with ingredients (start symbol), apply steps (productions), end with dish (terminal string).
Picture a pyramid - Type 0 at bottom (biggest, most powerful), Type 3 at top (smallest, most restricted). Each level is a subset of the one below.
Think of A → ε as a recipe that says 'add nothing' - the ingredient disappears but the recipe still works!
Think of A → B as a relay race baton pass - you pass from one non-terminal to another without producing terminals.

analogies.analogy: Like a recipe card - the ingredient (non-terminal) can be replaced by its components regardless of what's around it. 'Egg' can always become 'egg white + yolk', no matter where in the dish.
Non-terminals are like ingredients (flour, sugar) - can be broken down further. Terminals are like the final baked cake - you can't break it down anymore.
Like a family tree - great-grandparents at top (start symbol), children/grandchildren below. Each generation comes from applying rules to the previous one.
Like a person remembering nested brackets while reading code - each opening '(' pushes memory, each ')' pops to check match. The stack is their memory.
Like the sentence 'Time flies like an arrow' - it could mean time moves quickly OR 'Time' (the insects) 'like' (verb) 'an arrow'. Two meanings = ambiguous grammar.
Like assembling IKEA furniture - you always work on the leftmost piece first, following instructions in order, until everything is built.
Like solving a crossword - you fill in boxes by checking if combinations of letters work together, building from smaller words to complete strings.
Like standardizing shoe sizes - messy grammars get converted to normal forms so any parser can handle them consistently, like any shoe fits any size 10 foot.
